coredata.dc:title: The green synthesis of carbon quantum dots and applications for sulcotrione detection and anti-pathogen activities
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhen
Liu, Qinghao
Leng, Jiapeng
Liu, Hongyan
Zhang, Yuxin
Wang, Cundong
An, Wenqing
Bao, Chenning
Lei, Han
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we synthesized simple, cheap, and stable nitrogen (N)-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) from Moringa oleifera roots. The N-CQDs exhibited an intense blue fluorescence and a quantum yield (QY) of up to 43.4%. When excited at 350 nm, the highest generated wavelength was observed at 445 nm. These N-CQDs were then successfully used to detect sulcotrione (limit of detection = 2 μg/mL); the method was reliable and exhibited good feasibility for measurements in real samples. When the N-CQDs concentration was 11.0 μL/mL, inhibitory rates against the pathogens, Corynespora cassiicola and Phytophtora nicotianae were 82.8% and 75.3%, respectively. To investigate N-CQDs safety for plant growth, different concentrations were investigated using sorghum seedlings, with N-CQDs exhibiting very low toxicity toward plant growth. Thus, these findings provide a basis for the development of N-CQDs as green pesticides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Moringa oleifera roots
Sulcotrione
Inhibitory rates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of N-CQDs synthesis and applications.
(A) FTIR spectra of N-CQDs. (B) XRD pattern of N-CQDs. (C) TEM image of N-CQDs (Inset: size distribution of N-CQDs).
(A) XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs; (B-D) High-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks in N-CQDs, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs (inset: photographs taken under visible light and 365 nm UV light); (B) Emission spectra of progressively longer excitation wavelengths in 20 nm increments from 300 nm to 430 nm.

Stability of N-CQDs. (A) Effects of time on N-CQDs fluorescence intensity in distilled water, (B) Effects of pH on N-CQDs fluorescence intensity. (C) Effect of ionic strengths on N-CQDs fluorescence intensity (ionic strengths was controlled by different NaCl concentrations in aqueous solution). (D) Temperature effects on N-CQDs fluorescence emission. (E) Storage time on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs aqueous.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of different Sul concentrations. (B) The emission intensity ratios F/F0 were dependent on the concentration of added Sul (inset: photographs range from 0 to 20 μg/mL). (C) The fluorescence quenching effects of different pesticides.
Schematic diagram showing the fluorescence quenching principle of N-CQDs.

(A, B) The inhibitory activity of N-CQDs at 0, 11 and 22 μL/mL concentrations against (A) Corynespora cassiicola (inset: photographs under the UV light of 365 nm) and (B) Phytophtora nicotianae (inset: photographs under the UV light of 365 nm) (C) Sorghum seedlings; (a), (b), (c), (d), (e) were cultivated with different N-CQDs concentrations (0 μL/mL, 11 μL/mL, 22 μL/mL, 200 μL/mL and 500 μL/mL), respectively for 15 days (inset: photographs in Petri dishes).
Sul recovery in corn and tap water samples.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of King Saud University.

head.ce:title: The green synthesis of carbon quantum dots and applications for sulcotrione detection and anti-pathogen activities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Plant Protection, Henan Academy of Agriculture Sciences, Zhengzhou 450002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we synthesized simple, cheap, and stable nitrogen (N)-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) from Moringa oleifera roots. The N-CQDs exhibited an intense blue fluorescence and a quantum yield (QY) of up to 43.4%. When excited at 350 nm, the highest generated wavelength was observed at 445 nm. These N-CQDs were then successfully used to detect sulcotrione (limit of detection = 2 μg/mL); the method was reliable and exhibited good feasibility for measurements in real samples. When the N-CQDs concentration was 11.0 μL/mL, inhibitory rates against the pathogens, Corynespora cassiicola and Phytophtora nicotianae were 82.8% and 75.3%, respectively. To investigate N-CQDs safety for plant growth, different concentrations were investigated using sorghum seedlings, with N-CQDs exhibiting very low toxicity toward plant growth. Thus, these findings provide a basis for the development of N-CQDs as green pesticides.

ce:section.ce:para: Throughout the ages, the prevention of large-scale crop destruction by diseases and insect pests has been difficult for mankind. Pesticides are used to kill weeds, insect pests, and modulate disease to dramatically increase crop output and productivity in most countries. Thus, since their inception, pesticides have played major roles in agricultural development [1] and therefore remain enduring for the foreseeable future. In recent years, pesticide-use by farmers has increased dramatically, however a substantial part of pesticides are highly toxic as residual pesticides. Consequently, pesticides impact negatively on the environment, human health, and economic development, and these effects are likely to worsen. Therefore, it is particularly important to establish mechanisms that rapidly detect pesticide residues and develop green alternative pesticides.

With the normalization of crop import and export, pesticide residues [2] not only affect consumer health, but also impact on world trade and national reputations. Therefore, the establishment of rapid, accurate quantitative methods to quantitate pesticide residues is essential to assess the quality of agricultural products. Currently, several analytical methods have been established to detect pesticide residues in food [3]. These include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), fluorescent biological probes [4–6], field effect transistors [7], colorimetric analysis [8], chromatographic detection [9], electrochemical methods [10], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [11]. While these techniques are robust and highly sensitive for pesticide detection, they have several inherent disadvantages such as complicated steps, harmful chemicals, high costs, and the requirement for trained operators. These technologies limit their practical detection to some extent. In contrast, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be rapidly used as fluorescent probes and offer distinct advantages over conventional methods. Recently, the hydrothermal carbonization of pork [12], hawthorn [13], ginkgo biloba leaf extracts [14], and triflic acid treated palm was successfully applied to the synthesis of fluorescent CQDs, and served as probes to detect uric acid, chlortetracycline, fenitrothion/dithianon/dinoseb, and nitrophenol. Fluorescent probes of CQDs that incorporate natural substances exhibit good chemical stability, low toxicity, low costs, and direct detection. These features also make fluorescent probes of CQDs synthesized from natural substances ideal candidates for pesticide analysis and detection.

In recent years, research on CQDs antibacterial activity has increased. Du et al. [15] demonstrated that dual N-dopants CQDs, synthesized from glucose as a precursor, exhibited significant antimicrobial activities against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Escherichia coli. Gao et al. [16] discovered that beer yeast-modified fluorescent CQDs possessed major photo-induced bactericidal functions within 120 min under visible-light irradiation. Das et al. [17] discovered that N-CQDs exhibited captivating bacteriostatic activities against Gram-negative E. coli. Thus, CQDs have a considerable latent capacity for anti-bacterial functions.

In this study, Moringa oleifera roots were used as a carbon source, and intense blue fluorescent N-CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ). N-CQDs were also modified by citric acid and gallic acid. These N-CQDs were then successfully used to detect sulcotrione (Sul). We also studied the anti-pathogen activities of different N-CQDs concentrations against Corynespora cassiicola and Phytophtora nicotianae, and investigated the effects of N-CQDs on sorghum seedling growth. We believe our findings lay the foundation for increased CQD-use in pesticide research.

The pesticides, fenoxaprop-p-ethyl (Fpe), pinoxaden (Pin), messosulfuron-methyl (Mme), diniconazole (Din), nicosulfuron (Nic), promytryn (Pro), butachlor (But), mefenacet (Mef), flufenacet (Flu), propyzamide (Pro), acetochlor (Ace), sulfentrazone (Suf), bensulfuron methyl (Bme), florasulam (Flo), propisochlor (Pro), clodinafop-propargyl (Cpr), atrazine (Atr), fluroxypyr (Flu), triadimefon (Tri), Ethyl carbamate (Eca), carbendazim (Car), difenoconazole (Dif), diniconazole (Din), avermectin (Ave), buprofezin (Bup), imidacloprid (Imi), thiamethoxam (Thi), sulcotrione (Sul), and other chemicals were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All pesticides standard reagents met optical analytical requirements and all chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. All pesticides were freshly prepared in 50% methanol. Dialysis bags (3500 molecular weight cutoff (MWCO)), diethylenetriamine, HCl, NaCl and NaOH were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory.
Pathogens used in this study: Corynespora cassiicola and Phytophtora nicotianae came from Henan Key Laboratory of Crop Pest Control.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN) was used to examine N-CQDs size and morphology. Zeta potential analyses were performed using a Zeta Sizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of N-CQDs was conducted using a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo, USA). N-CQDs fluorescence spectra were processed on a Shimadzu 5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan), and UV–Vis absorption spectra were examined using a Unico 4802 Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). Centrifugation was performed using an H1850 high speed centrifuge (Changsha, China). X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). N-CQDs X-ray diffractometer (XRD) patterns were measured using a Bruker AXS D5005.

N-doped CQDs were generated as previously described, with some modifications [11]. Briefly, 1.5 g Moringa oleifera roots powder, 0.02 g citric acid, 0.02 g gallic acid, and 200 μL diethylenetriamine were added to 30 mL distilled water and 15 mL absolute alcohol. The mixture was sonicated for 15 min and then poured into a high-temperature reaction kettle to undergo a hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ for 10 h. When cooled to a certain temperature, a brown solution was obtained. Insoluble materials were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Then, the supernatant was collected and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO; 3500) for 36 h to eliminate small molecules. After this, pure N-CQDs were obtained and stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for later use.

The QY of N-CQDs was calculated using a previously reported comparative method, because it in water was calculable in reference to quinine sulfate with the QY of 53% in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (n = 1.33) at 350 nm excitation. The following equation was used to calculate the QY;
Q Y =
Q Y
S
n
2
I
A S
n
s
2
A
I S
where QY and QYS referred to the QYs of N-CQDs and the standard, respectively, A, AS, I, IS absorbance and standard, integrated fluorescence intensity of the compound and standard, respectively. Both n and nS represented the refractive indices of solvent samples and standards, respectively. For this study, n/nS = 1 [14,18].

For Sul detection, we carefully added 10 μL N-CQDs to 3 mL ultrapure water in individual containers, followed by different Sul concentrations. After a 5 min reaction, fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm and emission wavelength of 445 nm. Finally, a linear range graph and limit of detection data were generated. To assess N-CQDs selectivity, 20 pesticides were added to reaction systems to measure fluorescence intensity. The detection conditions were as described.

The proposed method was used to determine Sul levels in corn and tap water samples. Corn samples were recruited from the local market, and tap water samples came from the laboratory. Corn samples were ground to a powder. Sul (0, 0.2, 1, and 3 mg) was added to 1 g corn powder and 10 mL tap water after ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. The labeled corn sample was mixed with 50% methanol extract and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to generate a supernatant. All supernatants (spiked corn and water samples) were filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filters, after which they were diluted to assess method recovery and reproducibility.

The mycelial growth rate method was used to determine N-CQDs inhibitory activities on pathogens. 500 μL and 1 mL of N-CQDs were evenly mixed with sterile 45 mL PDA molten agar medium in a measuring cylinder, and poured into three Petri dishes (15 mL/plate). A control was established with water. Hypha discs were cut from the edge of the colony are cultured with 0.8 cm carrier and inoculated on the surface of the medium, with the mycelium downward. Three replicates were used for each concentration. Inoculated plates were incubated at 28 ℃ ± 1 ℃ for 5 days. After this period, colony diameters were measured and inhibition rates calculated [19,21].

The relative inhibition rate of the circle mycelium compared with the blank was calculated using the following equation: Relative inhibition rate (%) =
D s
- D
D s
× 100%, where Ds extended diameter of the circle mycelium in the blank, and D is the extended diameter of the circle mycelium in the test [20,22].

Sorghum seeds were soaked in deionized water and placed in the dark for two days to germinate. After this, sprouted seedlings were transferred to five Petri dishes containing filter paper in the bottom dish. Seedlings were cultivated in N-CQDs solutions containing 0 μL/mL (control), 11 μL/mL (1.1 vol%), 22 μL/mL (2.2 vol%), 200 μL/mL (20 vol%), and 500 μL/mL (50 vol%). Seedlings were grown at 25 °C–30 °C, 46% humidity, in 12 h/12 h light/dark cycle conditions. To measure plant growth, plant phenotypes were visually rated.

FTIR was used to determine interim surface functional groups on N-CQDs surfaces. FTIR spectra are shown (Fig. 2 A). Peaks at 3245 cm−1, which resulted from stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups [23], suggested abundant hydroxyl groups and amino teams on the surface. Peaks at 2983 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C–H, whereas C-N stretching vibrations occurred at 1010 cm−1 [24]. Peaks at 1703, 1557, and 1479 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, and N–H bonds, respectively [25–27]. XPS was also performed to further confirm the elemental composition and chemical state of N-CQDs. As shown (Fig. 3 A), the complete vary XPS spectra of N-CQDs clearly showed three typical peaks representing O1s (532 eV), N1s (405 eV), and C1s (287 eV) [13]. Their relative contents were ca.30.1%, 62.2% and 7.8% severally on the premise of calculations of the integral area. These results illustrated that N was successfully doped into CQDs. Specifically, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) was fitted to three Gaussian peaks at 284.95, 286.35, and 288.15 eV, and were attributed to CO, C–O, and C–C/C–N bonds, respectively [28]. The O1s spectra showed two peaks at 398 and 400 eV, which confirmed C–O/CO and O–H groups, respectively [29] (Fig. 3C). The N1s spectra showed two peaks at 398 and 400 eV, which were assigned to C–N and N–H functional groups [30], respectively (Fig. 3D). XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed the existence of the three elements, C, O and N, thereby indicating that N was successfully doped into CQDs.

TEM (Fig. 2C) data showed that N-CQDs exhibited a uniform morphology and were nearly spherical. The average particle sizes of N-CQDs (inset in Fig. 2C) was 3.6 nm, but this varied between 1.5 nm and 6 nm. An XRD plot analysis of powdered N-CQDs (powdered sample was derived after lyophilization) (Fig. 2B) showed a broad diffraction peak of plane at 2θ = 24.02. This suggested that N-CQDs possibly consisted of amorphous carbon [31].

N-CQDs were analyzed by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 4 A). Strong and wide absorption peaks at 352 nm indicated the presence of carbonyl or conjugated carbonyl groups. This may have been attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO bond [32,33]. The broad absorption peak may also have been due to N-CQDs passivation with functional groups [33].

The aqueous N-CQDs solution was yellowish under sunlight, but it emitted a bright blue fluorescence under at 365 nm under UV. As shown (Fig. 4B), the maximum excitation wavelength shifted from 300 nm to 430 nm in 20 nm increments as the emission spectra varied from 445 nm to 492 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of emission peaks first increased, then decreased [32–34]. When excited at 350 nm, the maximum emission intensity was observed at 445 nm. The change in excitation wavelength affected the shift of the emission spectrum. These data indicated that the photoluminescence spectra were dependent on the excitation wavelength.

To further study these fluorescence properties, we investigated different pH, temperature, time, and ionic strength conditions on N-CQDs fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs under permanent radiation (UV lamp) was constant for 120 min [35] suggesting a high resistance to photobleaching. N-CQDs fluorescence intensity also showed pH independent behaviors when the pH was altered between 2 and 11 (Fig. 5B). N-CQDs fluorescence intensity remained constant with altered NaCl concentrations and temperatures in aqueous solutions (Fig. 5C and 5D). Thus, according to the experimental results, their emission behavior is independent of pH, temperature, time and ionic strength. It is found that the synthesized of N-CQDs were freely and easily dispersible in water and were usable for 4 months without decreased intensity (Fig. 5E). The QY of the synthesized N-CQDs can reach 43.4 %. These findings will make them better used in optical field [36].

To explore N-CQDs selectivity toward Sul, 10-fold pesticide concentrations were used for selectivity investigations. For this, we used Fpe, Pin, Mme, Din, Nic, Pro, But, Mef, Flu, Pro, Ace, Sul, etc. As shown in Fig. 6C, pesticide addition other than Sul had no obvious effects on the reaction system. To evaluate method repeatability, N-CQDs were used to repeatedly measure the different pesticides under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 6C, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of each repeat was small suggesting the method had sensible repeatability for Sul. These data indicated that N-CQDs exhibited excellent selectivity for Sul.

To verify N-CQDs feasibility and relevancy to real life samples, we analyzed corn (Corn samples are purchased from the native market and crushed) and tap water samples [43,44]. As expected, the pretreatment samples did not detect Sul. After spiking Sul concentrations into samples, we repeated the analyses. As shown (Table 1 ), the recovery rates for corn and tap water samples ranged from 95% to 101.7% and 95%–105%, severally, and RSDs were in the range 0.7%–5% (n = 3). These results indicated that the N-CQDs methodology was reliable and feasible for Sul measurement in real samples.

Moreover, to verify the effects of N-CQDs as pesticides on plant growth, we used a hydroponics method to cultivate Sorghum seedlings in different N-CQDs concentrations. As shown in Fig. 8C, seedlings were cultivated in different concentrations; 0 μL/mL (control), 11 μL/mL (1.1 vol%), 22 μL/mL (2.2 vol%), 200 μL/mL (20 vol%) and 500 μL/mL (50 vol%). In the inset (Fig. 8C), seedling height was significantly higher than the control group when N-CQDs concentrations were 11 μL/mL (1.1 vol%), 22 μL/mL (2.2 vol%) and 200 μL/mL (20 vol%). At 500 μL/mL (50 vol%), root elongation was inhibited but limited effects were observed on seedling growth. N-CQDs contained N, C, O and H elements which may have promoted plant growth, but excessive levels may have inhibited root growth. The results indicated that N-CQDs displayed low toxicity toward plant growth, and promoted growth at concentrations that inhibited some pathogens. Thus, N-CQDs have potential applications and development prospects in the pesticide field.

We developed a simple one pot hydrothermal method to prepare N doped carbon dots with Moringa oleifera roots as a precursor, and used different analytical strategies to comprehensively characterize N-CQDs. N-CQDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence, high photo-bleaching resistance, high water solubility, and excitation-dependent emission behaviors. In addition, N-CQDs may serve as fluorescent probes for Sul, with good selectivity and a wide linear range. Importantly, N-CQDs were not only used for pesticide residue detection, but also exhibited strong anti-pathogen activities. During the study period, extremely low toxicity of N-CQDs in plants was verified in sorghum seedlings. Our findings provide a sound theoretical basis for CQDs in pesticide detection and anti-pathogen activities in plants. The results demonstrate that carbon quantum dots have considerable potential as green pesticides and warrant further study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21904121), and the authors gratefully acknowledge the support for this work from China Agriculture Research System of MOF and MARA and Henan Key Laboratory of Crop Pest Control (No. 201603).
